mappings.rationale: Directly covers the medieval period that is the primary subject of this section, from the fall of Rome through the late medieval period.
Directly covers subsections C and F on the Byzantine Empire from its origins through the Macedonian era, Iconoclasm, and decline.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England, the Danish invasions, King Alfred, and Anglo-Saxon political unification.
Directly covers the Norman Conquest mentioned at the end of subsection D.2.a and its transformative effects on English society and governance.
Relevant to subsections D and E covering Britain from the Carolingian era through the later Middle Ages, including the development of English monarchy and Parliament.
Directly relevant to subsection B on the eclipse of the Roman Empire and the transition to the Germanic successor states in the 5th century.
Directly covers subsection D.3 on the development of the Holy Roman Empire under the Saxon dynasty and later the Salian and Hohenstaufen rulers.
Relevant to the section's discussion of arts and intellectual life in the early and high Middle Ages, including Latin and vernacular literature.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the intellectual revival of the 11th and 12th centuries, Scholasticism, and developments in philosophy and theology.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the cultural functions of monasticism, Benedictine monasticism, the Cluniac order, and the mendicant orders.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the Byzantine Church, the schism with Rome (1054), and the ecclesiastical controversies between Eastern and Western Christianity.
Relevant to the section's discussion of late medieval church reform movements, the conciliar movement, and the Hussite movement that foreshadowed the Reformation.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of the Black Death (1347-50) and its economic, social, and political effects on late medieval Europe.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the Habsburg emperors, the division of Habsburg lands, and the enhancement of Habsburg power and influence in Europe by 1500.
Relevant to subsection E covering the rise of the Kievan Rus state, its Slavic-Varangian origins, and early Russian political institutions.
The section explicitly discusses the historical sources and historiographic problems for the study of medieval and Byzantine history.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of ruling dynasties — Carolingian, Capetian, Plantagenet, Hohenstaufen, Habsburg, Luxemburg, and others — and dynastic politics.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the feudal nobility, the ascendancy of princes and magnates, and the social orders of medieval Europe.
Directly relevant to subsection D.2.c on the Viking Age in Scandinavia, widespread raids and conquests, and the Norse invasions of the British Isles.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the early Italian Renaissance and developments in literature; Dante is a central figure of late medieval Italian literary culture.
Relevant to the section's coverage of late medieval English literary and cultural developments, including vernacular literature.
Relevant to the section's extensive discussion of medieval theology, Scholasticism, and the relationship between spiritual and temporal power.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Scholasticism, the intellectual revival of the 11th-12th centuries, and developments in philosophy and theology in the High Middle Ages.
Relevant to the section's mention of witchcraft as a topic in late medieval intellectual developments.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Scandinavian expansion into Iceland and Greenland, and Spanish and Portuguese overseas exploration and colonial policy in the late medieval period.
Relevant to the section's discussion of medieval religious culture, the role of the church in society, and the canonization of Louis IX in 1297.
Relevant to the section's discussion of medieval religious practices and the crusading era, which was partly motivated by the disruption of pilgrimage routes.
